hook.text: You can now read any Russian word. Let's use that skill immediately with the most useful words in any language — greetings. By the end of this lesson you'll know how to open and close every basic Russian interaction, in both formal and informal registers.

why.text: Russian has a strict formal/informal distinction that affects not just vocabulary but grammar. Getting this wrong marks you immediately as foreign and can come across as rude. Learning both registers from the start prevents bad habits — you'll know exactly when to use привет versus здравствуйте.

cards.title: Two registers: informal and formal
cards.body: Russian uses different greetings depending on the relationship. Informal (ты-register): for friends, family, children, peers your age. Formal (вы-register): for strangers, elders, authority figures, business contexts. When in doubt, use formal — it's never wrong.

examples.meaning: hello — formal, also used for groups
hello — formal to one person you know well (less common)

cards.title: Goodbye
cards.body: Like hello, Russian has formal and informal goodbyes. пока is casual and warm. до свидания is the safe default in any context.

cards.title: Time-of-day greetings
cards.body: Russian has specific greetings for morning, afternoon, evening, and night. They follow the same structure: добро(е/ый) + time of day. The adjective ending changes because it agrees with the noun — more on this in Unit 6. For now, learn them as fixed phrases.

questions.prompt: You're meeting your boss for the first time. Which greeting do you use?
questions.explanation: здравствуйте is the formal greeting — always correct with strangers, authority figures, and in professional settings.
пока (poka) = bye (informal). Use it with friends — not with your boss.

questions.prompt: What is the Russian greeting for 'good evening'?
questions.explanation: добрый вечер (dobry vecher) = good evening. вечер = evening (masculine).

cards.title: Pronouncing здравствуйте
cards.body: This word looks terrifying but is pronounced much more simply than it's spelled. The В in the consonant cluster ЗДРАВСТВ is usually swallowed — native speakers say 'ZDRA-stvuy-tye', not every letter. The ЛТ at the end also simplifies. Practise the rhythm: ZDRA-stvuy-tye (three beats).

examples.meaning: hello (formal) — say it in three beats

cards.title: Как дела? — How are you?
cards.body: The standard follow-up to привет is как дела? (kak dela? — how are things?). The standard reply is хорошо (good) or нормально (fine/normal). You won't use these in today's drill but you'll encounter them constantly — plant the pattern now.

questions.prompt: What is the informal way to say 'hi' in Russian?
questions.explanation: привет (privet) = hi (informal). Use with friends and peers.
до свидания (do svidaniya) = goodbye (formal). Literally 'until the meeting'.
доброе утро = good morning. Used until around noon.

questions.prompt: You're leaving a friend's house late at night. What do you say?
questions.explanation: спокойной ночи (spokoynoy nochi) = good night. Used when parting for the night.
добрый день (dobry den') = good afternoon. добрый = good (masculine), день = day.

questions.prompt: Your friend says привет. You reply informally. What do you say?
questions.explanation: Respond with the same register. Informal → привет.
здравствуйте (ZDRA-stvuy-tye) = hello (formal). The standard formal greeting.

rules: Informal register: привет / пока — friends, family, peers
Formal register: здравствуйте / до свидания — strangers, elders, professionals
When in doubt, use formal — it's never wrong
Time-of-day greetings: доброе утро / добрый день / добрый вечер / спокойной ночи
summary.nextLesson: Next: introducing yourself — your name, where you're from, and the two key pronouns я and ты/вы.

questions.prompt: Which greeting do you use with your boss?
What is the Russian for 'good morning'?
